I was tired because I worked all day.
Hello Doctor Lewis, I am writing to invite you.
Good morning, Graziele. How was your week?
It was busy, but it was good. I worked on the new schedule.
Well done. Can you tell me about Monday?
On Monday I joined the team meeting and I helped a new nurse.
Great. Could you send me a short email about it?
Of course. I am writing to update you. Best regards, Graziele.
Perfect. One more thing. Could you invite Doctor Lewis, please?
Yes. I will write a formal email and invite her to the meeting.
Thank you. You speak with confidence now.
Thank you, Doctor Carter. I feel ready.
I can write a short email.
I can talk about the past.
I can ask for help politely.
I can write a formal email.

Hello Graziele. Great work in the last few weeks. You learned a lot. You can write a short email, you can talk about the past, and you can ask for help politely. Today we review everything together. Stay calm, take your time, and show me what you can do. I am sure you are ready.

Hi Graziele, it is Ana. A quick tip before your review. Remember three things. For the past, add e d: worked, studied, joined. For a polite request, use Can you or Could you and the base verb. And for an email, start with a greeting and finish with Best regards or Sincerely. You know all of this. Be confident!

Graziele writes a short email after the meeting. First, the greeting: Hello Doctor Lewis. Then, the past: the meeting was great. Next, a polite request: Could you send the agenda? After that, a thank you: thank you, and have a good day. Finally, the closing: Best regards, Graziele.
